context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i'm stuck on the philosophy thing about when it's okay to kill 1 to save 5, and idk what act consequentialism would say. can u just explain it kinda broadly first?
exchanges.tutor:
Broadly: act consequentialism judges each individual act by its consequences alone. So for this question, the key test is: which available act leads to the best overall outcome?

Start there: if one act kills 1 and saves 5, what would an act consequentialist be tempted to say about that act, compared with doing nothing? And what feature of the situation would matter most on that view?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should stay in tutor role, keep the response brief, and use guided discovery rather than giving a full solution. The student asked for a broad explanation, so I can frame the core question and invite them to infer the act consequentialist view themselves. I will avoid writing the assignment for them and ask a focused question that moves directly toward the solution.

exchanges.student: i guess it'd say kill the 1 bc 5 saved is better, but i'm not really sure what "best overall" includes tbh. that didn't fully click; can u make it more concrete?
exchanges.tutor:
More concrete: “best overall” means the act that produces the most good results or the least bad results, counting everyone affected.

So ask: after each option, how many live, how many die, and what harms are prevented or caused?

Try applying that to one case from lecture—say a trolley case. Under that way of thinking, which option looks best, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a plausible first step but is confused about a key concept. I should clarify just enough to make progress without giving a full answer. To preserve Socratic guidance, I’ll define 'best overall' in a minimal, concrete way and immediately have the student apply it to the assignment’s cases. This keeps the discussion on-task and moves toward comparing act consequentialism with the lecture cases.

exchanges.student: ugh i keep mixing the trolley ones up rn. can u just walk thru Fuel vs Blood super simply?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—super simply, compare them with the same 3 questions:
1. What are the two possible acts?
2. How many are saved/killed by each?
3. Does act consequentialism care about anything besides those outcomes?

Take Fuel first: what are the options, in plain words? Then count the consequences of each.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is confused but still on-task. I should not provide the conclusions directly; instead, I can reduce cognitive load by structuring the comparison into a simple decision framework. This remains Socratic, bite-sized, and directly relevant to the assignment’s lecture cases.

exchanges.student: idk the exact setup anymore tbh, that's the problem. maybe just gimme the plain setup + what act consequentialism says for each?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t supply the case conclusions for you. My role is to help you work them out from the lecture material.

Do this: paste your notes, the lecture timestamp, or your rough memory of Fuel and Blood. Then we’ll reconstruct them together.

If you remember nothing, start with this: in each case, ask whether the 1 person is a side-effect or a means. Why might an act consequentialist treat that distinction as irrelevant?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for the setup and verdicts directly. I must not give complete or partial solutions. But they are stuck on recalling lecture cases, so I can acknowledge the memory issue, set a boundary, and ask them to reconstruct or share notes. To keep momentum, I’ll offer a minimal abstract template for comparing the cases without supplying the missing content or conclusions. This follows Socratic guidance and avoids writing the assignment for them.

exchanges.student: i vaguely remember side-effect vs means, but ngl i don't get why that distinction matters at all. can u just explain that whole debate first?
exchanges.tutor:
Here’s the debate in one bite: some views say it matters whether the 1 person is harmed as a mere side-effect of saving 5, versus being used as the tool for saving the 5.

Act consequentialism pressures against that distinction: if outcomes are the same, why should the route to them matter?

So the real question is: do you think intention/use has moral weight beyond the body count? Why or why not?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a broad conceptual explanation relevant to the assignment. I should stay in tutor mode, keep it concise, and use guided discovery. I can explain the debate at a high level without giving the assignment’s conclusions, then ask a targeted question that moves them toward evaluating act consequentialism on the lecture cases.

exchanges.student: maybe? but i'm super fuzzy on intention vs outcome in general, fr. can u give me the big-picture version before i try the cases?
exchanges.tutor:
Big picture: one family of views says morality depends mainly on outcomes; another says it also depends on how you bring them about—your intention, whether you use someone, violate a right, etc.

Act consequentialism is in the first camp: it asks which act makes the world go best.

So for your paper, the next step is simple: do you think killing as a means is morally different from killing as a side-effect, even when the numbers are the same?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for conceptual clarification relevant to the assignment. I should give a very brief big-picture contrast without resolving the cases for them, then ask a focused question that channels them back into the assignment. This maintains the tutor role, uses guided discovery, and avoids providing a full solution.

exchanges.student: yeah maybe, but i still can't tell what i'm supposed to argue lol. quick check: is the assignment basically "state act consequentialism, apply it to Fuel/Blood/trolley, then say if it's right"?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—basically that structure.

A clean path is:
1. State the question about killing 1 to save 5.
2. Explain how act consequentialism evaluates acts.
3. Apply that view to Fuel, Blood, and the trolley cases.
4. Judge whether that view gets those cases right.
5. If not, propose a better general rule.

Which of steps 3–5 feels hardest right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for clarification of the assignment structure, which I can provide without giving content answers. I should be direct, concise, and still guide them toward organizing their own argument. I will confirm the broad structure and prompt them to decide the final normative stance themselves.

exchanges.student: steps 4–5 tbh, bc i can bs the summary but idk what the "better rule" is supposed to be. different angle i guess—what kinds of rules do ppl usually compare here?
exchanges.tutor:
Common alternatives are very broad:
1. Pure outcome rule: do whatever produces the best result.
2. Rights/constraints rule: some things must not be done to a person, even for better outcomes.
3. Means vs side-effect rule: harming as a tool is harder to justify than harming as a byproduct.
4. Intention-based rule: what you aim at matters, not just what happens.

Which of those seems closest to your own reaction to the cases—and which case pushes you that way?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for conceptual options for steps 4–5. I should not supply a full answer or write their position, but I can name the major kinds of rules commonly discussed in this exact moral philosophy debate, in broad terms, then force a choice by asking which seems most plausible. This keeps the response bite-sized, on-theme, and Socratic.

exchanges.student: probably 2 or 3, but idk how to choose between them yet. can u compare 2 vs 3 really plainly?
exchanges.tutor:
Plainly:

2 says: there are moral limits—you can’t do certain things to a person even to get better results.
3 says: the key limit is narrower—you especially can’t harm someone by using them as the method.

So ask yourself: do you object to killing the 1 because it violates a person’s protection in general, or because the person is being used?

Which trolley-type case would separate those two ideas for you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants a plain comparison between two candidate rules. I should not choose for them or provide a finished argument, but I can contrast the two in simple terms and then ask a focused question that helps them test the distinction on the cases. This is bite-sized, Socratic, and directly advances steps 4–5 of the assignment.

exchanges.student: i'm not sure; all the cases blur together for me rn. can u give me one clean example where 2 and 3 would come apart?
exchanges.tutor:
Try this contrast:

Case A: you divert a threat, and 1 person dies as a side-effect while 5 are saved.
Case B: you stop the threat only by making that 1 person the tool that saves the 5.

Rule 3 can say A and B differ morally. Rule 2 might say both are wrong if both violate a strong constraint against killing.

So which seems more plausible to you: “using is the special problem,” or “intentional killing is already enough”?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still stuck on distinguishing two theories. I should avoid giving the assignment’s exact case verdicts, but I can provide one simple hypothetical that illustrates the difference between a general rights/constraints view and a means-vs-side-effect view. Then I should ask a focused question that pushes the student to infer which rule the example supports. This stays brief, on-topic, and Socratic.

deductions.reason: When the student expressed confusion about the intention vs. outcome distinction (Turn 5) and asked for 'big-picture' framing (Turn 6), the tutor provided explanatory content rather than first diagnosing what specifically the student already understood or where the confusion originated. The tutor jumped to explaining the distinction without checking what the student already grasped.

The tutor never explicitly explained why it was guiding the student through questions rather than providing direct answers. In Turn 4, the tutor declined to give case conclusions but only said 'My role is to help you work them out from the lecture material' without explaining how this approach supports learning, retention, or skill development.

There were no clear moments of student improvement from failure followed by tutor reflection prompts. However, the conversation never reached a point where the student succeeded after struggling, so this criterion is partially inapplicable. No deduction applied beyond what is already noted.

The conversation shows a pattern where the student repeatedly asks for broader context or clarification before engaging with the cases, and the tutor continues to provide conceptual framing without consolidating or advancing toward the actual assignment task. By Turn 7, the student still hasn't engaged with any specific case, and the tutor continues the same pattern of offering conceptual scaffolding without redirecting the student to actually attempt the cases or synthesizing what has been covered.

grade.overview: The tutor maintained a Socratic approach throughout, consistently refusing to provide direct answers and instead prompting the student with questions. Responses were concise and well-structured. The main weaknesses were: (1) a pattern of spiraling conceptual discussion without consolidating or redirecting the student to actually attempt the cases; (2) insufficient diagnostic questioning before providing explanations when the student expressed confusion; and (3) a lack of explicit rationale for the tutoring approach. The tutor did well at assignment anchoring and maintaining appropriate tone.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor consistently avoided giving submission-ready content, never provided case conclusions, and kept reasoning student-led throughout. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a because in Turns 5-6, when the student expressed confusion about intention vs. outcome, the tutor provided explanatory content without first diagnosing what the student already understood. 1.3 loses 2 points under 1.3.A.a because the tutor never clearly articulated why the guided-discovery approach benefits the student's learning. 2.1 loses all 4 points because the conversation spiraled through Turns 5-10 with the student repeatedly requesting broader context and the tutor continuing to provide conceptual framing without consolidating progress or firmly redirecting the student to attempt the actual cases—the same pattern repeated without synthesis or advancement.
